funding_source: No external funding. Internal research project.
citi_training_completion: Principal Investigator has completed CITI training in Human Subjects Research (Social/Behavioral Research).
protocol_description: This study examines how students in HR analytics courses evaluate the effectiveness of HR management tactics in situational judgment scenarios, and compares student ratings against HR professionals' ratings. We are testing whether students demonstrate variability in tactic ratings across scenarios, whether training shifts ratings toward evidence-aligned options, and how student ratings align with or differ from expert (HR professional) ratings.
population_description: Participants include two groups: (1) Undergraduate students enrolled in MNGT 425 (HR Analytics) and related Management and Marketing courses at Nicholls State University—the exercise is required for the course and supplements class time; and (2) HR professionals (e.g., HR managers, recruiters, compensation specialists) recruited through professional networks (e.g., LinkedIn, SHRM chapters, HR associations, professional contacts). All participants must be at least 18 years of age. There are no specific exclusion criteria beyond age requirements.
research_procedures:
The study involves the following procedures:

1. Informed Consent: Participants will read and provide electronic consent before beginning the study.

2. Participant Identification: Participants enter their first and last name; a participant ID is generated deterministically. No names are stored with research data.

3. HR Situational Judgment Test (HR SJT): Participants complete the online HR SJT, which presents 27 HR management scenarios (e.g., job analysis, job design, recruiting, selection, performance appraisal, sexual harassment, compensation, workplace accommodations, remote work, bona fide occupational requirements). For each scenario, participants rate the effectiveness of each available tactic on a 1–5 scale (Not effective to Highly effective).

4. Feedback: Participants receive a PDF report summarizing their ratings.

5. Debriefing: Participants receive information about the study purpose, HR decision-making concepts (evidence-based HR, situational judgment, legal compliance, cost-benefit analysis), and how their responses contribute to research. Contact information for the PI and IRB is provided.

All data collection will occur online through the servers hosted by Nicholls State University. The entire procedure takes approximately 45–60 minutes to complete. Student and HR professional ratings will be compared to identify how student evaluations align with or differ from expert (professional) ratings.
research_objectives:
The primary objectives of this research are:

1. To measure how students evaluate HR management tactics in situational judgment scenarios
2. To measure how HR professionals evaluate the same tactics, establishing an expert benchmark
3. To compare student ratings against HR professional ratings to identify gaps, alignments, and areas for educational improvement
4. To identify which tactics students and professionals rate as most and least effective across key HR domains
5. To examine whether training in evidence-based HR practices shifts student ratings toward professional norms (if pre/post design is used)
6. To contribute to understanding of HR education and situational judgment assessment in business curricula
research_questions:
1. How do students rate the effectiveness of HR tactics across scenarios (e.g., workplace accommodations, remote work, BFOQ)?
2. How do HR professionals rate the same tactics, and how do student ratings compare to professional ratings?
3. Are there significant differences in ratings between tactics within scenarios, and do these patterns differ between students and professionals?
4. Does training in evidence-based HR practices change students' tactic ratings toward professional norms?
5. What patterns of tactic preference emerge across HR domains, and where do students align with or diverge from professionals?
educational_justification:
This research serves educational purposes by:
- Providing students with hands-on experience in HR situational judgment assessment
- Teaching students about evidence-based HR management and its measurement
- Demonstrating research methodology and data collection procedures
- Contributing to students' understanding of HR decision-making in organizational contexts
recruitment_method: Participants will be recruited through two channels: (1) Students: Enrolled in MNGT 425 (HR Analytics); the exercise is required and supplements class time. Students may access the study through the PRAMS system or course materials. (2) HR professionals: Recruited through professional networks, including LinkedIn, SHRM (Society for Human Resource Management) chapters, HR professional associations, and direct outreach to HR practitioners. Recruitment materials will describe the study purpose, duration, and that participants will receive feedback reports.
recruitment_script:
Study Title: HR Situational Judgment Test: Evidence-Based HR Decision-Making

Description: You will complete an online HR Situational Judgment Test with 27 HR management scenarios. For each scenario, you will rate the effectiveness of different HR tactics. You will receive a feedback report. Your responses will be used for research on HR education and decision-making.

Duration: Approximately 45–60 minutes
Mode: Online (complete at your own pace)
Required for MNGT 425; supplements class time. All participants receive feedback reports.

Requirements: Must be 18 years of age or older
inclusion_criteria:
- Must be at least 18 years of age
- For students: Must be currently enrolled in MNGT 425 (HR Analytics) or related Management and Marketing courses at Nicholls State University
- For HR professionals: Must have current or recent professional experience in human resources (e.g., HR manager, recruiter, compensation specialist, HR generalist)
- Must have access to a computer or device with internet connection
- Must be able to complete web-based assessments and download PDF reports
exclusion_criteria:
- Under 18 years of age
- Unable to complete online assessments due to technical limitations
- Previously participated in this study (to avoid duplicate responses)
- For students: Not currently enrolled at Nicholls State University
- For HR professionals: No current or recent professional experience in human resources
benefits_to_subjects:
Participants will benefit from:
- Gaining insight into their own HR decision-making through immediate feedback reports
- Learning about evidence-based HR management and situational judgment through the debriefing
- Contributing to scientific understanding of HR education and assessment, including how student ratings compare to professional benchmarks
- Students: The exercise supplements MNGT 425 class time as a required course component
benefits_to_others:
This research may benefit:
- Other students and researchers interested in HR education and situational judgment
- Educators developing HR training programs
- Organizations seeking to understand HR assessment and evidence-based decision-making
benefits_to_society:
This research contributes to:
- Scientific understanding of HR situational judgment as a measure of decision-making
- Understanding of evidence-based HR practices in educational settings
- Development of better HR assessment and training methods
- Educational practices related to HR development in business curricula
payment_compensation: The exercise is required for MNGT 425 (HR Analytics) and supplements class time. No separate course credit or monetary compensation is provided. All participants (students and HR professionals) receive feedback reports.
costs_to_subjects: The only cost to participants is their time (approximately 45–60 minutes). There are no financial costs, physical risks, or other burdens associated with participation.
review_type_justification:
This study qualifies for exempt status under Category D (Educational settings, anonymous surveys/interviews) because:

1. The study involves minimal risk to participants (online situational judgment assessment)
2. All data collection is anonymous – students are identified by PRAMS ID numbers; HR professionals by anonymized session IDs
3. The study involves standard educational and business assessment procedures
4. No sensitive topics or vulnerable populations are involved
5. Participants are adults (18+) who can provide informed consent
6. The study does not involve deception, physical procedures, or collection of sensitive information
7. All procedures are standard in educational research settings

The study involves only anonymous data collection through online assessments, with no collection of identifying information beyond anonymized identifiers (PRAMS ID numbers for students; session IDs for HR professionals).
risk_statement:
This study involves minimal risk to participants. The primary potential risks are:

1. Psychological discomfort: Feedback may differ from expectations. However, all feedback is provided in a supportive, educational context with emphasis on learning and growth.

2. Time commitment: Participants invest approximately 45–60 minutes of their time. For students, the exercise supplements class time as a required course component.

3. Privacy concerns: While all data is anonymized, there is a minimal risk of data breach. However, no personally identifying information is collected beyond PRAMS ID numbers.

The risks in this study are no greater than those encountered in normal daily activities or routine educational assessments.
risk_mitigation:
Risks are mitigated through:

1. Comprehensive debriefing: All participants receive detailed debriefing explaining the study purpose, their performance, and evidence-based HR concepts in a supportive, educational context via the feedback reports.

2. Anonymization: All data is collected with PRAMS ID numbers only – no names, email addresses, or other identifying information is collected.

3. Voluntary participation: Participants can withdraw at any time without penalty.

4. Secure data storage: All data is stored securely on password-protected servers with access limited to the research team.

5. Support resources: Participants are provided with resources for further learning about HR management if they are interested.
data_collection_methods:
Data will be collected through the PRAMS online platform and the HR SJT web application using the following methods:

1. Participant identification: Participant ID generated from name (no names stored). Participant type (student vs HR professional) is recorded for comparison analysis.
2. HR Situational Judgment Test: Collected through web-based rating interface; responses stored in database (hr_sjt_sessions, hr_sjt_responses)
3. Session metadata: Start/end times, duration, incident-level and tactic-level ratings

All data collection occurs through secure, encrypted online forms. Students are identified by PRAMS ID numbers; HR professionals receive an anonymized session ID. No personally identifying information is stored with research data.
data_storage: All data will be stored securely on password-protected servers maintained by Nicholls State University. Data will be stored in the PRAMS system database and associated HR SJT tables, which use industry-standard encryption and security measures. Access to data is restricted to the principal investigator and authorized research team members. Data will be stored with PRAMS ID numbers only – no personally identifying information (names, email addresses, etc.) will be stored with the research data.
confidentiality_procedures:
Confidentiality is maintained through:

1. Anonymization: Participants are identified only by PRAMS ID numbers, which are already anonymized in the PRAMS system. No names, email addresses, or other identifying information is collected or stored with research data.

2. Secure access: Only the principal investigator and authorized research team members have access to the data, and all access is logged and monitored.

3. Data encryption: All data is stored using industry-standard encryption methods.

4. Limited data sharing: Data will only be shared in aggregated, anonymized form for publication purposes. Individual participant data will be shared but in anonymized form.

5. IRB compliance: All procedures follow IRB guidelines for data confidentiality and security.
data_retention: Research data will be retained for a minimum of 5 years after study completion, as required by IRB regulations. After this period, data may be retained longer for potential follow-up analyses or publication purposes, but will continue to be stored securely and confidentially. Anonymized data/analyses will be made available to the Open Science Framework.
data_access:
Access to research data is restricted to:
- Principal Investigator (Dr. Christopher Castille)
- Authorized research team members (if any)
- IRB members for review purposes (if requested)

Data will be accessed only for research analysis, publication preparation, and IRB compliance. All data access is logged and monitored.
consent_procedures:
Informed consent will be obtained electronically before participants begin the study. Students provide consent through the PRAMS system; HR professionals (recruited through professional networks) provide consent through the HR SJT assessment interface. The consent process includes:

1. Consent form display: Participants will see a comprehensive consent form explaining the study purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, and their rights as participants.

2. Consent questions: Participants must answer questions confirming they understand the study and their rights before proceeding.

3. Electronic consent: Participants provide consent by clicking "Yes, I consent to participate" after reading the consent form. They can also click on an "opt out" button and exit the study without penalty.

4. Right to withdraw: The consent form clearly states that participants can withdraw at any time without penalty.

5. Contact information: The consent form includes contact information for the principal investigator and IRB for questions or concerns.

The consent form is stored electronically in the PRAMS system as part of the research record.
data_monitoring_plan: Data will be monitored throughout the study period to ensure data quality, appropriate participant recruitment, and compliance with IRB protocols. The principal investigator will review data collection progress and address any issues that arise.
oversight_procedures: Study oversight will be provided by the Principal Investigator and IRB. Any adverse events or protocol deviations will be reported to the IRB immediately.
publication_plan: Results from this study may be prepared for publication. All publications will use aggregated, anonymized data only.
data_sharing_plan: Individual participant data will be shared but in anonymized form. Aggregated data may be shared through publication and open science repositories.
participant_access_to_results: Participants who are interested in learning about the study results can contact the principal investigator to request a summary of findings.
appendices_notes: Supporting documents include: HR SJT instrument description; consent form; recruitment copy. All materials are available for IRB review upon request.
irb_contact_notes: For questions or concerns about this research, participants can contact the Principal Investigator or IRB Office (Dr. Alaina Daigle, 985-448-4697).
